:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commitments vs. Parole Revocations

Prison populations are shaped by two primary groups: new court commitments, or individuals newly incarcerated for a criminal offense, and parole returns/revocations, those who return to prison for failing to meet the conditions of their parole. Understanding the relative proportion of these two groups is pivotal for evaluating the effectiveness of a state's criminal justice policies.



``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

A prisoner serving a “time” sentence may not be paroled until they have served at least 1/4 of that sentence. In Montana, a sentencing judge who imposes a state prison term of more than one year may also impose the restriction that the offender is ineligible for parole and participation in the supervised release program while serving the term. Separate sentences must generally run consecutively. If an offender receives a consecutive sentence after incarceration but before a parole ruling is made on the initial sentence, parole eligibility is determined by aggregating the original sentence and the consecutive sentence. However, if the parole ruling on the initial sentence has already been made, the offender will not be eligible for parole on the consecutive sentence until after the offender discharges the original sentence unless a hearing panel orders otherwise. Due to the fact that an offender may still be eligible for parole on the first sentence, the hearing panel may allow the second term to commence for the purposes of calculating parole eligibility. Note, however, that this does not shorten the length of the consecutive sentence.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 677 people were eligible for parole but still in prison for new crimes, with original sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years. This group made up 28% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 73% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 94% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 29% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes and with an original sentence length between 1-25 years.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Sentence Lengths

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated with an original sentence length of 10-24.9 years, accounting for 44% of this group.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Criminal Offenses

In 2020, the majority of the parole-eligible prison population serving sentences for new crimes with sentence lengths ranging from 1 to 25 years were incarcerated for offenses related to rape or sexual assault, accounting for 32% of this group.


``` ::: :::

Predicted Probabilities

Predicted probabilities: Controlling for offense and sentence length, predicted probabilities of release before/at eligibility date. Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date. Similarly, 80% of women and 74% of men were released before/on parole eligibility date

``` ::: :::

:::: ::: citation-section **Notes:** NCRP data is from 2020. The parole-eligible prison population only includes people with sentence lengths ranging between 1-25 years and people incarcerated for a new crime.
